source_alignment.subject: English
source_alignment.source_statement: Develop an appreciation and love of reading, understand increasingly challenging texts, and make inferences referring to evidence in the text.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This bridge pack moves pupils from primary inference to KS3 literary analysis using evidence, mood and authorial choices.

objective.child_goal: I can infer meaning in a literary extract and support it with evidence.
objective.statement: Make thoughtful inferences about character, mood and theme in literary extracts, using precise evidence.
misconceptions: Retells the extract instead of making an inference.
Selects evidence that is interesting but not relevant to the inference.
Makes a confident claim without explaining how the writer creates the effect.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The feeling or atmosphere created by the writing.
vocabulary.audio_script: Mood is the atmosphere the writing creates for the reader.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A tone dial changing the lighting around an extract.

vocabulary.child_explanation: An idea or feeling a word suggests beyond its basic meaning.
vocabulary.audio_script: A connotation is what a word suggests or makes us feel.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A word card casting idea shadows.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Explaining how evidence supports your interpretation.
vocabulary.audio_script: Analysis explains how the evidence proves your idea.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Evidence threads joining quote, inference and effect.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Choose one word that makes the scene feel tense.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Reactivate word-level evidence before whole-extract inference.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Writers choose words that shape the mood.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A short extract with selectable mood words.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Build a literature chain: quote, inference, effect.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce KS3 analysis as linked reasoning.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A strong literary answer links evidence to an inference and explains the effect.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Three linked analysis cards on a study desk.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: The corridor 'swallowed the last of the light'. What mood does this create?
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Swallowed suggests the darkness feels powerful, so the mood may be threatening.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The quote casts a shadow over the mood dial.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Pick the best quote to support the inference.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Practise relevance, not just quote spotting.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The best quote directly supports the idea.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Quote cards connect or fail to connect to an inference card.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which analysis explains how the word choice creates unease?
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Look for the answer that explains how the words affect the reader.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Three analysis bridges compete to link quote and effect.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Lyra why retelling the plot is not analysis.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I said what happened. Why is that not enough?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The companion holds a summary card beside an analysis chain.

manipulatives.purpose: Make literary inference, evidence relevance and analysis visible as a chain.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners highlight quotes, select mood/inference cards, connect effect bridges and write short analyses.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Line focus, text size controls, audio reading and static chain mode for reduced motion.

manipulatives.purpose: Let learners construct and test competing interpretations rather than hunt for one hidden answer.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners combine interpretation, quotation and effect cards, compare two plausible readings and strengthen the weaker chain with more precise evidence.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Offers line-by-line audio, keyboard card selection, plain-language prompts, optional sentence stems and a static side-by-side comparison with no time pressure.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave character, mood and theme inference after quote relevance is secure.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Identify words and phrases that create mood in short extracts.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: original extract + mood target + selectable spans
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Extracts must be original and suitable for Year 7 transition.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Choose evidence that directly supports a given inference.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: inference claim + quote cards + relevance feedback
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include plausible but weak quotes for diagnosis.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain how writer choices shape mood, character or theme.
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid formulaic analysis; allow precise alternatives.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Write concise analytical responses with quote, inference and effect.
Keep evidence precision and inference reasoning active through spaced review.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: one short extract + one evidence/inference decision
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep warm-up extracts short and accessible.

body.prompt: Highlight the phrase that best creates a threatening mood.
body.text: The corridor swallowed the last of the light, leaving the doorway like a black mouth.

value: swallowed the last of the light

hints: Look for words that make the place feel dangerous.
Which image makes the corridor seem alive?
question_variants.explanation: 'Swallowed the last of the light' and 'black mouth' create a threatening mood.

body.prompt: Which quote best supports the inference that the setting feels hostile?

expected_answer.value: the walls leaned inward

hints: Hostile means unfriendly or threatening.
Choose the quote that changes how the place feels.
question_variants.explanation: 'The walls leaned inward' makes the setting feel threatening or hostile.

body.prompt: Explain how the phrase 'the silence pressed against the windows' creates mood.
body.sentence_stem: The phrase creates a ... mood because...

hints: What feeling does 'pressed' suggest?
Explain how the phrase affects the reader.
question_variants.explanation: A strong answer explains that 'pressed' makes the silence feel heavy or oppressive.

body.prompt: Analysis bridge 35: Which comparison handles competing interpretations responsibly?

I keep the summer ticket
in a box that will not close.
Its ink has thinned to rain,
but the date still holds its ground.
choices: Both interpretations are equally strong without textual evidence.
The alternative is false because it is less obvious.

The inference that the speaker is reluctant to let a memory fade is supported by “the date still holds its ground”. However, The unclosing box may suggest the memory feels intrusive rather than simply precious. Evidence about whether the speaker revisits or hides the ticket would help discriminate between them.
Only the first interpretation can be true because literary evidence has one fixed meaning.

question_variants.explanation: “Almost definitely” is the most precise available span for character or speaker inference.

feedback.correct: The canonical evidence link supports “Almost definitely”. “Almost definitely” is the most precise available span for character or speaker inference.
feedback.repair: Put the inference beside each quotation and test whether the exact words prove it. Keep the smallest sufficient span.
feedback.evidence: Exact linked evidence: “Almost definitely”. Each span occurs verbatim in the original extract.
feedback.support_message: Use line focus, simplified text with the same reasoning, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC or adult scribing; no drag, speech, handwriting or speed is required.
feedback.retry: Relevant spans and completed links remain visible. Use one relevance clue and retry without penalty.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Retells the extract instead of making an inference.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner answers with plot summary only.
repair_sequence: Separate what happened from what it suggests.
Link one quote to one suggested meaning.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner gives an inference beyond retelling in a new prompt.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Selects evidence that is interesting but not relevant to the inference.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses a vivid quote that does not support the claim.
repair_sequence: Show claim and quote as two cards.
Test whether the quote proves the claim.
Choose a stronger quote and explain why.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner selects relevant evidence for a new claim.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm study-desk visuals and reduce animated shadows.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Use static quote, inference and effect cards.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read extracts aloud with sentence replay and vocabulary support.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use line focus, chunked extracts and glossary popovers.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Separate highlight, inference and analysis tasks.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow reread and draft time before short response.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Give partial credit for good evidence even when analysis needs repair.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach mood, evidence, suggests, connotation, effect and analysis.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires relevant evidence for literary inferences; secure requires explaining writer effect across unseen extracts.
evidence.parent_summary: Can make inferences about a literary extract and support them with precise evidence.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Highlights relevant quotes and explains how language choices create mood, character or theme.
next_actions: Add longer extracts once quote relevance is secure.

qa.notes: Year 7 literary inference pilot reaches 220 variants with three curated questions preserved semantically and structurally unchanged and 217 deterministic review candidates. All generated micro-extracts are original NexusLearn text with explicit rights metadata across prose, poetry and drama-like writing. Coverage includes precise evidence, explicit and inferred meaning, mood, character, theme, imagery, structure, writer effect, evidence sufficiency, overclaiming, competing defensible interpretations and discriminating additional evidence. Auto-marked items use exact-span links; all 43 short responses require complete rubrics and teacher/adult moderation. SEND routes, pressure-free retrieval and optional reviewed ElevenLabs readings are included; browser TTS is prohibited.
